summary: In this CVPR 2021 keynote, Andrej Karpathy outlines Tesla's vision-only approach to autonomous driving. He explains why Tesla abandoned radar in favor of a pure vision stack, detailing the unscalability of lidar and HD maps, the power of offline auto-labeling, and the iterative 'Data Engine' used to source edge cases from the fleet to train massive neural networks.

principles.statement: Vision alone is perfectly capable of depth sensing.
principles.rationale: Humans drive using vision, proving it contains the necessary information. With a massive, clean, and diverse dataset, synthetic neural networks can match or exceed radar's depth and velocity estimation.
principles.source_quote: vision alone is actually in our is our finding that is perfectly capable of depth sensing

principles.statement: Lidar and high-definition maps are fundamentally unscalable.
principles.rationale: Collecting, building, and maintaining high-definition lidar maps globally is incredibly expensive and difficult to keep up to date. A general vision system that interprets the scene for the first time is harder to build but infinitely more scalable.
principles.source_quote: it's actually quite unscalable to uh to actually collect build and maintain these high definition lidar maps

principles.rationale: Owning the vehicle, sensors, data sourcing, annotation, training cluster, and custom silicon allows a team to co-design the entire stack without being held back by third parties.
principles.source_quote: everything is very vertical integrated in the team and i think that's pretty incredible because um you get to really co-design an engineer at all the layers of that stack

principles.statement: Shift processing burden to training and labeling time.
principles.rationale: Test time in the car has strict latency requirements. Heavy lifting, like 3D reconstruction and complex tracking, should be done offline during training and auto-labeling to simplify the tasks required at test time.
principles.source_quote: you want to do as much a training time and labeling time as possible and then you want to figure out what are the tasks i really need that test time

frameworks.name: The Data Engine
frameworks.when_to_apply: When improving a neural network's performance on rare edge cases and difficult real-world scenarios.
steps: Train a neural network on a seed dataset.
Deploy the network silently in customer cars in 'shadow mode' (not connected to vehicle control).
Use specific triggers to source inaccuracies or disagreements between the network's predictions and legacy systems/driver behavior.
Send flagged clips to unit tests and an offline auto-labeling pipeline.
Incorporate the newly labeled hard examples into the training set.
Clean the dataset asynchronously and repeat the loop.
frameworks.source_quote: we spin this loop over and over again until the network basically becomes incredibly good

frameworks.name: Offline Auto-Labeling
frameworks.when_to_apply: When generating massive, clean, and diverse datasets for complex 3D object tracking that humans cannot manually annotate at scale.
steps: Collect raw sensor data from the fleet.
Process the data offline without strict latency requirements.
Use heavy, non-deployable neural networks and expensive offline optimization.
Leverage the benefit of hindsight (looking at future frames) to stitch together occluded tracks.
Involve humans only for cleaning, verification, and editing.
frameworks.source_quote: because you're offline and you're trying to just annotate a clip you have a large number of benefits that you don't typically have if you're at test time

mental_models.name: Meat vs. Silicon Computer
mental_models.description: Viewing human drivers as 'meat in the control loop' with high latency, limited awareness, and distractions, compared to 'silicon computers' which have 360-degree situational awareness, low latency, and zero distractions.
mental_models.example: Arguing for autonomous driving by pointing out that humans get into accidents because they are 'meat computers' controlling high-kinetic-energy metallic objects.

mental_models.name: Sensor as a Crutch
mental_models.description: The idea that an inferior sensor in a fusion stack stops contributing useful data and instead acts as a crutch that injects noise and masks the true capabilities of the dominant sensor.
mental_models.example: Removing radar from Tesla vehicles because vision became 100x better, and radar was causing phantom braking by falsely identifying bridges as stationary objects.

heuristics: When radar and vision disagree, vision has much more precision so better to double down on vision than do sensor fusion.
If you have a large, clean, diverse dataset and train a large enough neural network, success is guaranteed.

signature_quotes.text: we have these metallic objects traveling incredibly quickly and with very high kinetic energy and we are putting meat in the control loop
signature_quotes.context: Describing the fundamental problem with human-driven transportation.

signature_quotes.text: it should be possible to replace the meat computer with a silicon computer and get a lot of benefits out of it
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the motivation behind autonomous driving.

signature_quotes.text: if you have a sensor that is dominating the other sensor and so much better then the other sensor is actually starting to like really contribute it's actually holding me back and it's really starting to attribute noise
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why Tesla removed radar to rely purely on vision.

signature_quotes.text: everything that happens uh happens for the first time there in the car based on the videos from the eight cameras that surround the car
signature_quotes.context: Contrasting Tesla's vision-first approach with competitors' reliance on pre-mapped HD lidar maps.

anti_patterns.description: Relying on Lidar and High-Definition Maps for autonomy.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It is unscalable to collect, build, and maintain high-definition maps for millions of customers globally. It requires expensive infrastructure to keep up to date.

anti_patterns.description: Sensor fusion with an inferior sensor.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: When one sensor (vision) vastly outperforms another (radar), the inferior sensor introduces noise (e.g., false stationary objects) and masks the inaccuracies of the primary sensor, leading to wasted engineering effort on a flawed stack.

anti_patterns.description: Relying purely on humans to annotate 3D driving data.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It cannot reach the scale (millions of hard examples) or precision required for depth and velocity estimation. Offline auto-labeling is necessary to achieve the required volume and quality.
